panel.origin: The best possible hand in standard poker: A-K-Q-J-10 all of the same suit. It cannot be beaten. The odds of being dealt one in five cards are about 1 in 649,740.
panel.mixology: After three hours of folding, the river completed his royal flush. The table went silent, then erupted — the dealer admitted she'd never seen one in ten years on the floor.
panel.examples: A 20th-century American term highlighting the 'royalty' of the court cards topping the hand. It's technically the highest straight flush, and in video poker it's the jackpot, typically paying 4,000-to-1.

panel.origin: Five cards in sequence, all the same suit (for example 6-7-8-9-10 of hearts). The second-strongest hand, beaten only by a higher straight flush or a royal.
panel.mixology: He flopped a straight flush and slow-played it perfectly, letting his opponent bet into a hand that was already unbeatable.
panel.examples: The name simply combines a 'straight' (a run of cards) and a 'flush' (one suit). Both terms predate poker, drawn from older card games and, in 'flush', ultimately from the idea of cards 'flowing' together in one suit.

panel.origin: Four cards of the same rank, such as four kings, plus any fifth card. Also called 'quads.' A very strong and rare hand.
panel.mixology: 'I've got quads,' she said flatly, flipping over four nines. It's one of the few hands you can announce without fear of a bluff.
panel.examples: 'Of a kind' meaning 'of the same type' dates to the 16th century. The shorthand 'quads' is modern poker-room slang, mirroring 'trips' for three of a kind.

panel.origin: Three cards of one rank plus two of another (for example three queens and two sevens). Ranks below four of a kind but above a flush.
panel.mixology: He filled up on the turn — pocket pairs becoming a full house — and suddenly his cautious early checks looked like a trap.
panel.examples: Originally called a 'full hand' in 19th-century play; 'full house' became standard by the early 1900s. The image is of a hand that is 'full' — every card contributing to the combination.

panel.origin: The best possible hand given the cards on the table, which by definition cannot be beaten at that moment.
panel.mixology: 'I have the nuts here,' he announced, knowing no combination of remaining cards could top his straight. The only question was how much he could extract.
panel.examples: The term likely comes from old gambling: a player would place the nuts of his wagon wheels on the table as a bond he literally could not flee, signifying a bet he was certain to win.

panel.origin: Two cards of the same rank dealt face-down to a single player as their private 'hole' cards, such as two aces ('pocket rockets').
panel.mixology: She'd been dealt pocket kings — the second-best starting hand — and had to decide whether to raise big or disguise their strength.
panel.examples: 'Pocket' refers to the hidden hole cards held privately, as if in one's pocket, distinct from the shared community cards on the board.

panel.origin: A hand containing two cards of one rank and two of another, plus a fifth card — for example two jacks and two fives.
panel.mixology: Two pair looks strong to a beginner but is a classic trap hand; he overcommitted with jacks and fives and ran straight into a set.
panel.examples: A plainly descriptive term as old as the ranked-hand system of poker itself, which crystallized in mid-19th-century America.

panel.origin: A side card that doesn't form part of a pair or combination but decides the winner when two players hold otherwise equal hands.
panel.mixology: They both had a pair of aces, but his king kicker beat her queen — the side card nobody thinks about until it costs them the pot.
panel.examples: From the sense of a card that 'kicks in' to break a tie. The usage is mid-20th-century American poker slang, now universal in the game.

panel.origin: A hand that isn't yet winning but could become strong if the right card arrives — for example four cards to a flush, 'drawing' to the fifth.
panel.mixology: He was on a flush draw with one card to come, doing the math: nine cards could complete it, giving him roughly a one-in-five shot.
panel.examples: 'Draw' comes from the act of drawing additional cards, central to older draw-poker variants; the 'drawing hand' is one still hoping to improve.

panel.origin: Betting every chip you have on the table in a single commitment. You can't be forced out, but you can't win more than your stake either.
panel.mixology: With the tournament on the line he pushed all-in, and the whole table leaned forward — one card now decided whether he went home or doubled up.
panel.examples: The phrase is older than poker, meaning fully committed with nothing held back. Its poker use spread through 20th-century cardrooms and became iconic with televised tournaments.

panel.origin: Checking (passing the action) to feign weakness, then raising after an opponent bets — a trap to extract more chips or win the pot.
panel.mixology: She check-raised the river, and you could see her opponent's confidence drain; the check had invited exactly the bet she wanted to punish.
panel.examples: A descriptive compound of the two actions it chains. Once considered unsporting and even banned in some genteel home games, it's now a fundamental tactic.

panel.origin: The ratio of the current pot size to the cost of a call, used to decide whether a draw is mathematically worth chasing.
panel.mixology: The pot offered 4-to-1 and his draw would hit about 1-in-5 of the time — the pot odds said call, even though his gut said fold.
panel.examples: A 20th-century formalization as poker strategy grew more mathematical, popularized by theorists like David Sklansky who put numbers to the game's instincts.

panel.origin: Betting or raising with a weak hand to make opponents fold better ones. A pure bluff wins only if everyone folds; a semi-bluff also has cards that can improve.
panel.mixology: Daniel Negreanu's real genius isn't bluffing — it's sniffing out others' bluffs, making 'hero calls' that look impossible until the cards turn over.
panel.examples: From Dutch 'bluffen' (to boast or deceive), entering English in the early 1800s as poker spread along the Mississippi. Poker is widely credited with the word's modern meaning.

panel.origin: A 'continuation bet': betting on the next street after you raised before the cards came, continuing the story that you have a strong hand.
panel.mixology: He raised before the flop, missed it completely, but fired a c-bet anyway — most of the time, the story alone was enough to take the pot.
panel.examples: A modern term from the online-poker era of the 2000s, when systematic, aggressive post-flop betting was codified and named.

panel.origin: Entering a pot by just calling the minimum rather than raising — often seen as passive or weak play.
panel.mixology: He limped in with a marginal hand, and the sharks at the table smelled it instantly; limping told them exactly how little he held.
panel.examples: The image is of slipping into the pot 'limply,' without the strength of a raise. It's 20th-century cardroom slang, now slightly pejorative among serious players.

panel.origin: The third bet in a sequence — a re-raise of an initial raise. A key aggressive move that applies pressure before the flop.
panel.mixology: She 3-bet from the blinds, flipping the script; the original raiser now had to defend out of position against a hand he couldn't read.
panel.examples: Bet-counting terminology ('2-bet', '3-bet', '4-bet') emerged from online poker in the 2000s, importing the precise language of limit poker into the no-limit boom.

panel.origin: Deliberately playing a strong hand weakly — checking or just calling — to disguise its strength and lure opponents into betting.
panel.mixology: He flopped a full house and slow-played it, checking meekly until the river, by which point his opponent had bet himself broke.
panel.examples: A descriptive term for 'playing slowly,' the opposite of fast, aggressive betting. It's the strategic mirror image of the bluff: hiding strength rather than faking it.

panel.origin: A bet made with a strong hand to get called by weaker ones — betting 'for value' rather than to bluff opponents out.
panel.mixology: He had the best hand and needed to get paid, so he sized his value bet just small enough to look temptingly callable.
panel.examples: A modern strategic term distinguishing bets made to profit from a winning hand ('value') from bets made to make better hands fold ('bluffs').

panel.origin: The marker showing who is the nominal dealer, and the most powerful seat — that player acts last after the flop, with the most information.
panel.mixology: On the button with position locked up, she could watch everyone else act first and pounce, controlling the size of every pot.
panel.examples: Named for the literal button (or 'buck') passed around the table to mark the dealer — the origin, too, of 'the buck stops here.'

panel.origin: Forced bets posted by the two players left of the button before any cards are seen — the small blind and big blind — that seed the pot.
panel.mixology: He was sick of the blinds eating his stack and decided to defend his big blind aggressively for once, refusing to be bullied.
panel.examples: Called 'blind' because the bets are made before seeing any cards — committing money 'blindly.' Blinds replaced older 'ante' structures to force action.

panel.origin: The seat immediately to the right of the button — the second-best position, often used to steal the blinds before the button can act.
panel.mixology: From the cutoff he raised to steal, betting that the button and blinds would all fold and hand him the pot uncontested.
panel.examples: Named because a raise from this seat can 'cut off' the button player from entering the pot cheaply, seizing late position from them.

panel.origin: The seat immediately left of the big blind — the first to act before the flop, and the worst position, requiring the strongest hands.
panel.mixology: Under the gun with a marginal hand, he folded without a second thought; acting first against eight players is no place for hope.
panel.examples: A 19th-century American idiom meaning 'under pressure,' borrowed into poker for the player forced to act first with no information.

panel.origin: Acting after your opponent on every betting round, giving you the advantage of seeing what they do before you decide.
panel.mixology: Being in position let her control the hand entirely — every time he checked, she could bet; every time he bet, she already knew.
panel.examples: A straightforward strategic term; 'position' as the key concept of who acts when became central as poker theory matured in the late 20th century.

panel.origin: An unconscious physical or behavioral cue that reveals information about a player's hand — a trembling hand, a glance, a change in breathing.
panel.mixology: The classic tell: weak when strong, strong when weak. He stared down his opponent, who suddenly couldn't stop talking — and folded the winner.
panel.examples: From 'to tell,' meaning to reveal. The poker sense was popularized in the 20th century and cemented by films and Mike Caro's famous 'Book of Tells.'

panel.origin: The full set of hands a player could plausibly hold in a given situation, rather than guessing one specific hand. Thinking in ranges is core to modern strategy.
panel.mixology: Instead of asking 'does he have aces?' she thought in ranges — the whole spread of hands he'd play this way — and bet against all of them at once.
panel.examples: A modern, mathematically-minded concept that reshaped expert play in the 2000s, shifting poker thinking from single-hand reads to probability distributions.

panel.origin: Your share of the pot based on your hand's statistical chance of winning — if you'd win 60% of the time, you have 60% equity.
panel.mixology: He had the worse hand but more equity to improve, so getting his chips in wasn't a mistake — the math was on his side even when he lost.
panel.examples: Borrowed from finance, where 'equity' means a stake or share. Poker adopted it to express a player's mathematical claim on the pot.

panel.origin: 'Game Theory Optimal' play — a mathematically balanced strategy that can't be exploited, even if opponents know exactly what you're doing.
panel.mixology: The young pros studied GTO solvers for hours, learning to balance their bluffs and value bets so precisely that no opponent could ever read them.
panel.examples: Rooted in the game theory of John von Neumann and John Nash, GTO entered poker vocabulary in the 2010s with computer solvers that approximated unexploitable play.

panel.origin: The total money a player has set aside specifically for poker. Sound 'bankroll management' means risking only a small fraction in any one game.
panel.mixology: He had the skill but blew through three bankrolls before learning the hardest lesson: you can play perfectly and still go broke if you bet too big.
panel.examples: A 19th-century American term for a roll of banknotes; in poker it came to mean the dedicated fund that keeps a player in action through inevitable swings.

panel.origin: Calling a big bet with a relatively weak hand because you've read your opponent as bluffing — a risky, glory-or-disaster move.
panel.mixology: Everyone at the table would have folded, but she made the hero call with bottom pair, having decided his story simply didn't add up.
panel.examples: Modern poker slang; the 'hero' is the protagonist making a brave, against-the-odds call, a usage popularized by online forums and televised play.

panel.origin: Losing a hand you were heavily favored to win because your opponent hit an unlikely card. The source of poker's greatest agony.
panel.mixology: He had aces against tens, all the money in, and watched a third ten land on the river — the textbook bad beat that haunts players for years.
panel.examples: A 20th-century gambling term; 'beat' for a defeat is old, and 'bad' captures the unfairness of losing as a big favorite. Endless 'bad beat stories' are a poker cliché.

panel.origin: A weak, inexperienced, or losing player — the one the stronger players hope to find at the table. 'If you can't spot the fish, you are the fish.'
panel.mixology: The regulars circled the loud tourist dropping chips on every hand; in poker terms, the fish had just sat down at the wrong table.
panel.examples: Gambling slang likening an easy mark to a fish to be caught. The companion term 'shark' completes the predator-and-prey imagery of the cardroom.

panel.origin: An emotional state of frustrated, reckless play, usually after a bad beat — chasing losses and abandoning strategy. 'On tilt.'
panel.mixology: After three brutal beats he was visibly on tilt, shoving all-in on hopeless hands, donating his stack to the very players who'd cracked him.
panel.examples: Borrowed from pinball, where nudging the machine too hard triggers 'TILT' and the game locks up — a perfect metaphor for a player whose judgment has gone haywire.

panel.origin: A skilled, often deceptively unassuming player who preys on weaker opponents. The opposite of a fish.
panel.mixology: He played the clueless amateur for an hour, then revealed himself as a shark, having quietly mapped every player's tells while pretending to fumble.
panel.examples: Gambling slang since at least the 1800s, from 'sharper' (a cheat or schemer). The fish-and-shark pairing makes the cardroom food chain vivid.

panel.origin: A derogatory term for a bad player ('donkey'), and 'donk bet' for the unconventional move of betting into the previous round's aggressor.
panel.mixology: He made a donk bet into the raiser, and the table couldn't tell if it was a genius trap or, more likely, exactly the donkey play it looked like.
panel.examples: Short for 'donkey,' implying a stubborn, foolish player. It rose with online poker in the 2000s, both as an insult and, in 'donk bet,' a named tactic.

panel.origin: The fifth and final community card. By extension, 'to be rivered' is to lose when that last card gives an opponent the winning hand.
panel.mixology: He was ahead the whole way and got rivered — that final card pairing the board and snatching the pot from his hands at the last instant.
panel.examples: Possibly from riverboat poker, where a cheat caught dealing from the bottom could be thrown in the river — or simply the last card 'flowing' out. The origin is debated.

panel.origin: A hand where two players both make very strong holdings and one inevitably loses a huge pot — bad luck rather than bad play.
panel.mixology: Full house against four of a kind: a pure cooler. Nobody could have folded, and he shrugged it off — there's no lesson in a hand you couldn't escape.
panel.examples: Modern poker slang, from the idea of a situation that 'cools off' a hot run of cards. It absolves the loser: a cooler is misfortune, not a mistake.
